title: HM Treasury

infobox.value: [1 Horse Guards Road](/wiki/1_Horse_Guards_Road "1 Horse Guards Road")

(often shortened to **HM Treasury**, the **Treasury** or **HMT**) is the [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom")’s economic and finance ministry, maintaining control over the United Kingdom's public spending and setting its economic policy. It is one of the smallest UK government departments by staff count, but is widely considered the most powerful.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**His Majesty's Treasury** (often shortened to **HM Treasury**, the **Treasury** or **HMT**) is the [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom")’s economic and finance ministry, maintaining control over the United Kingdom's public spending and setting its economic policy. It is one of the smallest UK government departments by staff count, but is widely considered the most powerful.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HM_Treasury&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Anglo-Saxon governments were skilled in collecting, auditing, and managing their cash revenues. However, there is no evidence of a distinct administrative department. Royal treasures were kept at Winchester, but the crown's wealth was likely kept more flexibly.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) For example, [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor") kept some treasure at his palace in Westminster.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![Wooden tally sticks](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Tally-Sticks.jpg/330px-Tally-Sticks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tally-Sticks.jpg)

Wooden tally sticks used by the Normans as receipts for money.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The first signs of an administrative department arrive with the Normans. Following the [1066 Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), the Exchequer was an event held twice a year, at Easter and [Michaelmas](/wiki/Michaelmas "Michaelmas"). The purpose of these meetings was to make judgements about the collection and usage of the royal treasury. These events were separate from the royal treasury, which stored the royal wealth.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The earliest known official of the treasury was Henry the Treasurer. It is likely that Henry the Treasurer partook in the Exchequer events.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The name Exchequer came from the chequered cloth that was spread over the counting‐table at these events.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) By 1154, the Exchequer was a fully-fledged administrative institution based in Westminster.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The royal treasury became part of the Lower Exchequer, whilst the twice-yearly meetings formed the Upper Exchequer.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

By the 1400s, the significance of the Exchequer was undermined. Much of its administrative and treasury functions were handled personally by [Edward IV](/wiki/Edward_IV "Edward IV").[[15]](#cite_note-15) This trend continued. [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") would personally check, sign, and draft the accounts of the Crown.[[16]](#cite_note-16) By the early 1500s, this situation was reversed by [Thomas Cromwell](/wiki/Thomas_Cromwell "Thomas Cromwell"). Authority was restored to the Exchequer, and four new government departments were created to handle the state finances. In 1554, these new departments were merged with the Exchequer.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However by the late 1500s, under [James I of England](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England"), debts mounted and the treasury reserves began to dwindle.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In an attempt to control the spiralling debt, Treasurer [Lionel Cranfield](/wiki/Lionel_Cranfield,_1st_Earl_of_Middlesex "Lionel Cranfield, 1st Earl of Middlesex") briefly imposed strict control over spending on the Crown. This would be the first attempt by a Treasurer to impose control over the sovereign. He was later impeached under the guise of corruption.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)

> All Treasurers, if they do good service to their masters, must be generally
> hated.

[James I of England](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Wenceslas_Hollar_-_Whitehall_from_the_river_%28State_3%29.jpg/330px-Wenceslas_Hollar_-_Whitehall_from_the_river_%28State_3%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wenceslas_Hollar_-_Whitehall_from_the_river_(State_3).jpg)

Whitehall Palace seen from the Thames in the mid-1600s.

In the 1640s, the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") broke out. The Exchequer collapsed and finance fell to ad hoc Parliamentary and Royal committees. In 1654, [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") re-opened the Exchequer but left its medieval structure intact.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) At the 1660 [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration"), a Lord Treasurer resumed charge, but Parliament won a permanent role in the treasury. Thus, the Treasury was caught between its sovereign-court origins and Parliament's growing authority.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Parliament had promised [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") £1.2m per year. However, Charles was unable to extract such funds due to the poor financial state of the country.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In 1667, without consulting Parliament, Charles decided to replace the [Lord Treasurer](/wiki/Lord_High_Treasurer "Lord High Treasurer") with a small board of officials. He wanted the "rougher hands of younger, more energetic men to wrest control of the country's finances."[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Treasury would now be run not by a single person, but by a board. One notable member was [Sir George Downing](/wiki/Sir_George_Downing,_1st_Baronet "Sir George Downing, 1st Baronet"), who brought lessons from [Dutch finance](/wiki/Financial_history_of_the_Dutch_Republic "Financial history of the Dutch Republic") to England.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Sir_George_Downing-24) Although England still lagged behind the Dutch in trade, its economy was growing rapidly. The board ended the practice of [tax farming](/wiki/Tax_farming "Tax farming") (selling the right to tax citizens to third parties) in favour of collecting the taxes directly. This led to an increase in tax revenue and an expansion of the Treasury's staff.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

However, due to heavy naval spending in the [Anglo–Dutch wars](/wiki/Anglo%E2%80%93Dutch_wars "Anglo–Dutch wars"), government debt ballooned. [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") halted all debt repayments, in what would become known as the [Stop of the Exchequer](/wiki/Stop_of_the_Exchequer "Stop of the Exchequer"). This substantially undermined trust in the government's finances. In response, Parliament began tightly controlling how government money was spent.[[26]](#cite_note-26) That control vanished in 1685, when Parliament gave [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") nearly £2 million a year with no restrictions. After the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688, the new king, [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), was deliberately kept short of permanent income so that he remained dependent on Parliament. The revolution also led to the development of new financial tools like the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), the [Land Tax](/wiki/Land_Tax_(England) "Land Tax (England)"), and [government bonds](/wiki/Government_bond "Government bond").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The new government made reforms to the Treasury too. [Henry Guy](/wiki/Henry_Guy_(politician) "Henry Guy (politician)") was a Member of Parliament as well as a Treasury Secretary. However, he was later disgraced for having taken a bribe. New rules were created to prevent all office-holders from sitting in the Commons. This effectively created a divorce between civil servants and the Commons.[[28]](#cite_note-28) By [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain")'s death in 1714, the Treasury operated independently from the monarch, and could shape its own direction.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Parade_at_Horseguards.jpg/250px-Parade_at_Horseguards.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parade_at_Horseguards.jpg)

Kent's Treasury is the squared stone building in the middle.

At the beginning of the 1700s, the Treasury held power in some areas but lacked it in others. It held significant power in tax collection. 17,000 civil servants gathered taxes for the government. Parliament would allow the Treasury to handle day-to-day management of tax collection. However, the Treasury rarely questioned government spending decisions.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In 1736, a new Treasury building was commissioned. The new building would be physically connected to [No. 10](/wiki/10_Downing_Street "10 Downing Street") via passageways. The Treasury was now firmly under the control of Parliament.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/James_Gillray_-_Opening_of_the_Budget%2C_-_or_-_John_Bull_Giving_His_Breeches_to_Save_His_Bacon_-_B1981.25.1021_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg/250px-James_Gillray_-_Opening_of_the_Budget%2C_-_or_-_John_Bull_Giving_His_Breeches_to_Save_His_Bacon_-_B1981.25.1021_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Gillray_-_Opening_of_the_Budget,_-_or_-_John_Bull_Giving_His_Breeches_to_Save_His_Bacon_-_B1981.25.1021_-_Yale_Center_for_British_Art.jpg)

A satirical image depicting the 'Opening of the Budget'

The term [budget](/wiki/Budget_of_the_United_Kingdom "Budget of the United Kingdom") emerged during this period. It originally meant a small pouch in French ([bougette](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bougette "wiktionary:bougette")). In a pamphlet in 1733, [Sir Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole") is depicted satirically as a quack doctor opening his bag (or bougette) of tricks. The joke stuck, and the term shifted from meaning the container to meaning the financial plan itself.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The power of the Treasury would increase in the late 1700s. The [Civil List](/wiki/Civil_list "Civil list") was the main way Parliament funded the monarch's household budget. By the late 1770s, [King George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") had accumulated considerable debt. By 1782, Parliament increased the Civil List to £900,000. For the first time, it also demanded detailed scrutiny of Civil List accounts. This was a significant change. The Sovereign's own expenditure would now be scrutinised by the Treasury.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

In 1776, the [American Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") was signed. Some blame the Treasury for the rebellion.[[35]](#cite_note-35) At the time, the Treasury had more officers in America than any other department.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Additionally, after the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") in Europe, government debt had soared. This pushed the Treasury to tighten customs, change pay incentives, and lower duties to curb smuggling. In 1767, Chancellor [Charles Townshend](/wiki/Charles_Townshend "Charles Townshend"), introduced the tax legislation which would ultimately ignite the rebellion. However, others emphasise that the Treasury acted alongside the [Board of Trade](/wiki/Board_of_Trade "Board of Trade"), the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)"), Parliament and other government bodies.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In the 1800s, the Treasury became the government's hard brake. The Treasury relied on the 'power of the purse' to enforce [Gladstone's economic restraint](/wiki/Gladstonian_liberalism "Gladstonian liberalism"). This led to constant clashes with the other departments.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The Treasury's control would deepen when the Exchequers of the [Great Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") were consolidated in 1817.[[39]](#cite_note-CA1816-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Parliament had complete control of government finances, and the Treasury emerged as the centre of financial discipline.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In the 1850s, a shift in the staffing of the Treasury occurred.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The [Northcote–Trevelyan Report](/wiki/Northcote%E2%80%93Trevelyan_Report "Northcote–Trevelyan Report") (1854) set the stage for this change. Recruitment for the Treasury was reorganised around merit and fixed pay. The Treasury shifted from hiring through royal connections to a small, selective elite. Stricter exams raised entry standards. However, public school connections still mattered. By 1914, public school and Oxbridge men dominated the ranks of the Treasury.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/HM-treasury-note-10-shillings-bradbury-B.jpg/250px-HM-treasury-note-10-shillings-bradbury-B.jpg)](/wiki/File:HM-treasury-note-10-shillings-bradbury-B.jpg)

A Bradbury note issued by the Treasury.

During the First World War, the Treasury attempted to stabilise finances. It closed the Stock Exchange, paused many payments, and issued new paper money like the [Bradbury note](/wiki/Bank_of_England_10_shilling_note "Bank of England 10 shilling note").[[44]](#cite_note-treasurynotes1-44) Ideologically, the Treasury shifted from [Gladstonian](/wiki/Gladstonian_liberalism "Gladstonian liberalism") prudence to [Keynesian](/wiki/Keynesian_Revolution "Keynesian Revolution") management of jobs and prices.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Troops_from_the_Grenadier_Guards_constructing_sandbag_defences_around_government_buildings_on_the_corner_of_Horse_Guards_Road%2C_Great_George_Street_and_Birdcage_Walk%2C_London%2C_May_1940._H1588.jpg/330px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Troops_from_the_Grenadier_Guards_constructing_sandbag_defences_around_government_buildings_on_the_corner_of_Horse_Guards_Road,_Great_George_Street_and_Birdcage_Walk,_London,_May_1940._H1588.jpg)

Troops from the Grenadier Guards constructing sandbag defences around the Treasury.

In the Second World War, [Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz") bombing damaged the Treasury building. This led the Treasury to move to [1 Horse Guards Road](/wiki/Government_Offices_Great_George_Street "Government Offices Great George Street"), where it remains to this day.[[46]](#cite_note-history-46) For the first time, the 1941 Budget used national accounts. National accounts track the entire economy's income and output. The Treasury now had a complete picture of the economy. In 1947, [Sir Stafford Cripps](/wiki/Stafford_Cripps "Stafford Cripps") moved economic planning into the Treasury. The Treasury now managed the budget, national accounts, and the pound.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Between 1947 and 1968, the Treasury would see a shrinking of power. Parliamentary reviews from 1957 to 1958 resulted in the Plowden Report.[[49]](#cite_note-49) This report encouraged more delegation to departments. Another report in 1968 criticised the Treasury's role as being too broad. The Treasury would hand over some functions to a newly created [Civil Service Department](/wiki/Minister_for_the_Civil_Service "Minister for the Civil Service").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In 2022, the Permanent Secretary, Sir [Tom Scholar](/wiki/Tom_Scholar "Tom Scholar"), was sacked by Chancellor [Kwasi Kwarteng](/wiki/Kwasi_Kwarteng "Kwasi Kwarteng") and Prime Minister [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss"). Contemporary reporting described it as unprecedented in Treasury history.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HM_Treasury&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Functions")]

HM Treasury is the United Kingdom's economic and finance ministry. It manages the public finances and leads the government's economic strategy, with responsibility for fiscal policy (including the Budget and tax policy) and for controlling public expenditure across departments. It allocates resources through Spending Reviews and sets departmental budgets to deliver ministers' priorities and value for money. It also sets the strategic framework for the tax and customs system and works with HM Revenue & Customs on policy design and delivery.[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)

In macroeconomic management, the Treasury sets the fiscal framework and coordinates fiscal policy with the Bank of England's operationally independent monetary policy. The government's fiscal rules and the role of the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) provides independent forecasts that accompany the Budget and assess performance against the rules.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

The Treasury also leads policy for the UK's financial services framework and legislation, while specialist regulators supervise firms and markets. In particular, HM Treasury is responsible for financial services policy and for taking the relevant legislation through Parliament; prudential and conduct supervision is carried out by the Prudential Regulation Authority and the Financial Conduct Authority within the statutory framework (notably the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000).[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)

As the government's central finance ministry, the Treasury works across Whitehall to ensure policies are affordable, consistent with the fiscal framework and deliverable within spending limits. Public spending plans and tax measures are adjusted at fiscal events (such as the annual Budget) in light of the economic outlook, Parliamentary scrutiny and statutory rules. The Treasury has additional offices in [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington"), [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") and [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich").[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)
